---
title: "Germany Economic Analysis"
author: "Group Germany"
date: "2024-12-03"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(tmap)
library(sf)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(gifski)

setwd("C:/Users/User/OneDrive/Рабочий стол/FINAL")
```

## SECTION 1

In the first section, we make an analysis of the spatial distribution of
GDP and Research and Development (R&D) expenditures across Germany’s
regions from the years 2017 to 2019. The main goal here is to see the
pattern of how the geographical distribution of these two variables
correlates. The hypothesis here is whether both variables have a
positive impact on each other, while having also a strong correlation.
Thus by conducting this analysis, we can draw conclusions regarding the
relationship between these two variables, in our case for Germany.

**Data Preparation:**

Data preparation is one of the most crucial steps. After cleaning,
tidying, and transforming the data, the final dataset used for analysis,
referred to as deu, has the following relevant variables:

-   **GDP:** Regional GDP in millions of euros for each region from 2017
    to 2019.

-   **R&D:** Regional R&D expenditures in millions for each region.

-   **Region:** The name of the federal state or region in Germany
    (total of 16 regions).

-   **Year:** The period (2017, 2018, 2019).

-   **geometry:** Spatial data for each region of Germany, containing
    values in multi polygons.

We also used the sf package to convert the dataset into a spatial
object, deu, to improve mapping so it could be more precise.

## PRE-PROCESS DATA

```{r, warning=FALSE, include=TRUE}
############################ First stage

#We start the task by importing first dataset in xlsx format of one of key variables in our 
#research, which is GDP by region in mil EU.

gdp <- read_xlsx("gdp.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet 1", skip=7)

#remove unnecessary columns
gdp <- gdp %>%
  select(-starts_with("..."))

#remove NA values
gdp <- na.omit(gdp)

#renaming for appropriate name
gdp <- rename(gdp, Region = TIME)

#selecting relevant years
gdp <- select(gdp,'Region','2017','2018','2019')

#Tidying the data by making it longer and more organized. 
#Year should be considered as value
gdp <- gdp %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = "2017":"2019",
    names_to = "Year",                           
    values_to = "GDP_mln")

#creating a list of region names corresponding for each subregion
region_to_state <- list(
  "Baden-Württemberg_" = c("Stuttgart", "Karlsruhe", "Freiburg", "Tübingen"),
  "Bayern_" = c("Oberbayern", "Niederbayern", "Oberpfalz", "Oberfranken", "Mittelfranken", "Unterfranken", "Schwaben"),
  "Berlin_" = "Berlin",
  "Brandenburg_" = "Brandenburg",
  "Bremen_" = "Bremen",
  "Hamburg_" = "Hamburg",
  "Hessen_" = c("Darmstadt", "Gießen", "Kassel"),
  "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern_"="Mecklenburg-Vorpommern",
  "Niedersachsen_" = c("Braunschweig", "Hannover", "Lüneburg", "Weser-Ems"),
  "Nordrhein-Westfalen_" = c("Köln", "Münster", "Detmold", "Arnsberg"),
  "Rheinland-Pfalz_" = c("Rheinhessen-Pfalz","Koblenz","Trier"),
  "Saarland_" = "Saarland",
  "Sachsen_" = c("Dresden", "Chemnitz", "Leipzig"),
  "Sachsen-Anhalt_" = "Sachsen-Anhalt",
  "Schleswig-Holstein_" = "Schleswig-Holstein",
  "Thüringen_" = "Thüringen")

#unlisting the previous list
region_to_state_vec <- unlist(region_to_state)

#creating new column of regions matching each subregion
gdp$FederalState <- names(region_to_state_vec)[match(gdp$Region, region_to_state_vec)]

#separating columns values to remove unecessary signs
gdp <- gdp %>%
  separate(FederalState, into = c("FederalState", "number"), sep = "_")

#removing unecessary columns and renaming federal states to regions for comfort use
gdp <- gdp %>%
  select(-"number", -"Region") %>%
  rename(Region = FederalState)

#grouping region and year variable to sum up GDP values for each unique region
gdp <- gdp %>%
  group_by(Region, Year) %>%
  summarize(GDP_mln = sum(GDP_mln))

############################ Second stage

#Now we import the second variable, which is regarding Research and Development 
#expenditures in mil EU
rd <- read_xlsx("R&D expenditure and staff.xlsx")

#remove NA values, extra columns, and filter for EUR mn
rd <- na.omit(rd)
rd <- filter(rd, Unit=='EUR mn')
rd <- select(rd, -c("Unit","Type"))

#transforming columns of regions to a longer format
rd <- rd %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = "Baden-Württemberg":"Thüringen",
    names_to = "Region",
    values_to = "R&D expenditure"
  )

#transforming unique sector names to columns to make dataset wider
rd <- pivot_wider(rd,names_from='Sector', 
                  values_from='R&D expenditure')

#to show needed variables
rd <- select(rd, Year, Region, Total)

#Merging two variables' datasets into one
gdp_rd <- merge(gdp, rd , by=c('Region', 'Year'))

############################ Third stage

#Now we import the shapefile containing Germany map data
deu <- read_sf('gadm41_DEU_2.shp')

#renaming for appropriate name
deu <- rename(deu, Region=NAME_1)

#Merging every dataset into one
deu <- merge(deu, gdp_rd, by='Region')

#renaming for appropriate name
deu <- rename(deu, "R&D_mln"=Total)

#to show needed variables
deu <- select(deu, Region, Year, GDP_mln, "R&D_mln", geometry)
deu_all<-select(deu, Region, Year, GDP_mln, "R&D_mln", geometry)

#convert to sf format for more precision
section_1_data <- st_as_sf(deu)

```

## VIZUALIZATION OF SECTION 1

**Visualization of Regional Distributions:**

To properly understand the spatial distribution, we visualized the
distribution of GDP and R&D spendings across Germany using choropleth
maps. The first map represents **Regional GDP**, and the second one
shows **R&D expenditures**.

-   **GDP Map:** The choropleth map for GDP reveals regions with higher
    GDP values, typically represented by darker shades, in which
    dark-blue for the highest and light-yellow for the lowest values.
    The southern regions, such as **Bavaria** and **Baden-Württemberg**,
    and also some parts of western Germany stand out with darker shades,
    indicating higher economic development. In comparison, the eastern
    regions show lighter shades, representing lower GDP values.

-   **R&D Map:** The distribution of R&D expenditures follows a similar
    pattern, with dark-red for the highest and light-yellow for the
    lowest numbers of expenditure.

```{r, warning=FALSE, include=TRUE}
plot1 <- tm_shape(section_1_data) +
  tm_polygons(col = "GDP_mln",
              style = "pretty",          
              palette = brewer.pal(9, "YlGnBu"), 
              border.col = "black",      
              lwd = 0.2) +
  tm_facets(along = "Year", free.coords = FALSE) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title.size = 2,
    main.title.position = "center",
    main.title.fontface = "bold",
    legend.width = 4,
    legend.title.size = 1,          
    legend.text.size = 1, 
    legend.outside = TRUE,
    legend.position = c("left","bottom"),
    bg.color = "#E0FFFF",
    frame = FALSE) +
  tm_compass(
    type = "8star",                    
    position = c("left", "bottom"),      
    size = 2                           
  )


#Plotting the second variable
plot2 <- tm_shape(section_1_data) +
  tm_polygons(col = "R&D_mln",
              style = "pretty",          
              palette = brewer.pal(9, "YlOrRd"), 
              border.col = "black",      
              lwd = 0.2) + 
  tm_facets(along = "Year", free.coords = FALSE) +
  tm_layout(
    main.title.size = 2,
    main.title.position = "center",
    main.title.fontface = "bold",
    legend.width = 4,
    legend.title.size = 1,          
    legend.text.size = 1, 
    legend.outside = TRUE,
    legend.position = c("left","bottom"),
    bg.color = "#FFEFDB",
    frame = FALSE) +
  tm_compass(
    type = "8star",                    
    position = c("left", "bottom"),      
    size = 2                           
  )
```

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#Animating maps of two variables
tmap_animation(plot1, filename = "gdp.gif", delay = 90)
tmap_animation(plot2, filename = "rd.gif", delay = 90)
```

## RESULTS OF VIZUALIZATION

## GDP per region

![](gdp.gif)

## R&D expenditures

![](rd.gif)

**Interpretation of Patterns:**

The pattern of the darker and lighter regions across both maps after
plotting them suggests a relatively strong correlation between **R&D
investment** and **GDP performance** across German regions.
Specifically:

-   **Southern Regions:** **Bavaria (Bayern)** and
    **Baden-Württemberg**, with their well-developed industrial progress
    and infrastructure, show high values for both GDP and R&D
    consistently for 3 years.

-   **Eastern Regions:** The regions in eastern Germany surrounding
    Berlin, have the opposite case with lower R&D investments and GDP
    values. This pattern may suggest that these regions faced some
    challenges in the economy from 2017 to 2019, which can be possibly
    explained by historical factors in that region.

-   **Western Regions:** These regions in western Germany demonstrate
    quite high indicators for variables’ distributions while being
    approximately lower than southern Germany and higher than eastern
    Germany.

## CONCLUSION OF SECTION 1

Overall, the maps showcase very close geographical patterns between
**GDP** and **R&D expenditures**, with higher values for both variables
concentrated in the southern and western regions of Germany. The visual
patterns shown by choropleth maps indicate that higher R&D investments
correlate with better economic output, in our case measured in GDP.
However, the lighter shades in the eastern regions show the opposite
situation with lower values. Nevertheless, conducting a choropleth map
analysis suggests that these results generally support our proposed
hypothesis, within our case. These observations support the thought that
investment growth in research and development, specifically in
light-shaded regions of Germany, is required to make the whole of
Germany having relatively the same colors.

**References**

[1] Ozturk, F. O. (2022). The Interaction Among R&D Expenses and
Economic Growth Evidence From EU Transition
Economies.<https://www.igi-global.com/gateway/chapter/301884>

[2]     Moustapha, A., Yu, Q. (2020). Innovation Effect Through Research
and Development on Economic Growth in 35 OECD
Countries.<https://typeset.io/pdf/innovation-effect-through-research-and-development-on-3602rf67uh.pdf>

[3]     D. Gaidhani, N. Nair, S. Jadhav, S. Rastogi and V. Mrudula
Bhimavarapu (2022), Impact of Research and Development on The Gdp Growth
Rate of Nations: Evidence Using Panel
Data.<https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9786420>

[4]     Islamoglu, E. (2017). Causality Relationship of Research &
Development Investment Expenditures and Gross Domestic Product:
Comparison of Turkey and Selected Oecd
Countries.<https://www.researchgate.net/publication/340875546_Causality_Relationship_of_Research_Development_Investment_Expenditures_and_Gross_Domestic_Product_Comparison_of_Turkey_and_Selected_Oecd_Countries>

[5]     Meo, SA., Al Masri, AA., Usmani, AM., Memon, AN., Zaidi, SZ.
(2013), Correction: Impact of GDP, Spending on R&D, Number of
Universities and Scientific Journals on Research Publications among
Asian
Countries.<https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0066449>

## SECTION 2

Building upon the analysis in Section 1, this section introduces the
**student rate of higher education** as an additional variable to assess
the role of education in economic development. Following the work of
Oketch et al. (2014), education is recognized as a significant driver of
economic growth through the enhancement of human capital. Grant (2017)
considered that the human element consists of three most important parts
and education is one of them. This section also looks for the
correlation between education and R&D expenditures, testing the
hypothesis that regions with stronger R&D investment and better economic
development have more students. However, the results show a more complex
reality where this hypothesis does not consistently hold.

```{r, message=FALSE, include=FALSE}

library(viridis)
library(sf)
library(VIM)
library(mice)
library(foreign)

```

## DATASETS

```{r, warning=FALSE, include=FALSE}

#open necessary file and choose sheets 

#our key variables are GDP, education(number of students) and R&D expenditure

education <- read_excel("Population (aged 15 and over).xlsx", sheet = 'Used')
RD<-read_excel('R&D expenditure and staff.xlsx')
gdp<-read_excel('gdp.xlsx',sheet='Sheet 1',skip=7)
germany<- st_read('gadm41_DEU_1.shp')

######################
#rename for merging later and select needed columns
germany<-rename(germany, Region=NAME_1)
germany<-select(germany,4,12)


```

## DATA PREPARATION

We found data from various sources and prepared it for analysis. The
final dataset, all_data, includes the following variables: **GDP:**
Regional GDP in millions of euros (2017–2019). **Students:** Number of
students in each region (2017–2019). **R&D:** Regional R&D expenditures
in millions (2017–2019). **Region:** The name of Germany’s 16 federal
states. **geometry:** Geometric data in polygon format for spatial
visualizations.

R packages such as sf, dplyr, and tmap were used for data processing,
visualization, and spatial analysis. The dataset was converted into an
sf object to enable precise mapping and visual analysis.

## PRE-PROCESS GDP DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE, include=TRUE}
#same for gdp and also omit NAs
gdp<-select(gdp,TIME,starts_with('20'))
gdp<-rename(gdp,Region=TIME)
gdp<-na.omit(gdp)
gdp<-select(gdp,'Region','2017','2018','2019')


########################################################
#create a list of regions and then assign the necessary subregions to them 
region_to_state <- list(
  "Baden-Württemberg_" = c("Stuttgart", "Karlsruhe", "Freiburg", "Tübingen"),
  "Bayern_" = c("Oberbayern", "Niederbayern", "Oberpfalz", "Oberfranken", "Mittelfranken", "Unterfranken", "Schwaben"),
  "Berlin_" = "Berlin",
  "Brandenburg_" = "Brandenburg",
  "Bremen_" = "Bremen",
  "Hamburg_" = "Hamburg",
  "Hessen_" = c("Darmstadt", "Gießen", "Kassel"),
  "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern_"="Mecklenburg-Vorpommern",
  "Niedersachsen_" = c("Braunschweig", "Hannover", "Lüneburg", "Weser-Ems"),
  "Nordrhein-Westfalen_" = c("Köln", "Münster", "Detmold", "Arnsberg"),
  "Rheinland-Pfalz_" = c("Rheinhessen-Pfalz","Koblenz","Trier"),
  "Saarland_" = "Saarland",
  "Sachsen_" = c("Dresden", "Chemnitz", "Leipzig"),
  "Sachsen-Anhalt_" = "Sachsen-Anhalt",
  "Schleswig-Holstein_" = "Schleswig-Holstein",
  "Thüringen_" = "Thüringen")
#unlist previous list to create vector
region_to_state_vec <- unlist(region_to_state)
#creating new columns for region(Federal_Sates) using previous vector and match them with regions
gdp$Federal_State <- names(region_to_state_vec)[match(gdp$Region, region_to_state_vec)]

#cleaning Federal States column
gdp<-gdp%>%
  separate(Federal_State, into = c("Federal_State", "number"), sep = "_")
#remove unnecessary column
gdp$number=NULL

########################################################
# change GDP data to long format, group, and summarize
gdp<-pivot_longer(gdp,cols=starts_with('20'),
                            values_to='Value',
                            names_to='Year')
                         
gdp<-gdp%>%
  group_by(Federal_State,Year)%>%
  summarize(TotalGDP=sum(Value,na.rm=TRUE))
#return to wide format
gdp<-pivot_wider(gdp,names_from='Year',
                            values_from='TotalGDP')
#rename
gdp<-rename(gdp,Region=Federal_State)


```

## PRE-PROCESS EDUCATION DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#######################################################
#filtering education variable
education<-filter(education,Sex=='Total')
education$Sex<-NULL
education$Total<-NULL
education<-rename(education,'Trade_and_technical_school_certificate'='Trade and technical school certificate')
education<-rename(education,'Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR'='Specialised and engineering school certific. (GDR)')
education<-rename(education,"Doctor"="Doctor's degree")
education$Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR<-as.numeric(education$Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR)
education$Year<-as.numeric(education$Year)

#check at missingness
aggr(education)
#impute data where were NA
imp<-mice(education,method='pmm',maxit=10,m=1)
education<-complete(imp,action='long',include=FALSE)

education$.imp<-NULL
education$.id<-NULL
#sum u Student rate per 10,000 people and select
education<-education%>%
  mutate(Total=Trade_and_technical_school_certificate+Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR+Bachelor+Master+Diploma+Doctor)
education<-education%>%
  select(Year,Region,Total)
#change format
education<-education%>%
  pivot_wider(names_from='Year',
              values_from='Total')

```

## PRE-PROCESS R&D DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}

#########

RD<-na.omit(RD)
RD<-filter(RD,Unit=='EUR mn')
RD$Unit<-NULL
RD$Type<-NULL

RD<-pivot_longer(RD, cols=('Baden-Württemberg':'Thüringen'),
                 values_to='Value',
                 names_to='Region')

RD<-pivot_wider(RD,
                names_from='Sector',
                values_from='Value')

RD$Gov<-RD$`Government, private non-profit institutions`
RD$`Government, private non-profit institutions`<-NULL
RD<-select(RD,Year,Region,Total)

RD<-pivot_wider(RD,
                names_from='Year',
                values_from='Total')

```

## MERGING ALL DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#merge data of all variables
all_data<-merge(gdp, education, by='Region')
all_data<-merge(all_data,RD, by="Region")
all_data<-merge(all_data, germany, by='Region')
#rename for comfort use
all_data<-all_data%>%
  rename(
    GDP_2017 = `2017.x`,
    GDP_2018 = `2018.x`,
    GDP_2019 = `2019.x`,
    Students_2017 = `2017.y`,
    Students_2018 = `2018.y`,
    Students_2019 = `2019.y`,
    RD_2017=`2017`,
    RD_2018=`2018`,
    RD_2019=`2019`)

#####
#change to spatial data frame for mapping
all_data<-st_as_sf(all_data)
tmap_mode('plot')

#adding new dataset for population
population<- read_excel("demo_r_d2jan$defaultview_page_spreadsheet.xlsx", sheet='Sheet 1', skip=9)
population<- select(population, -starts_with('...'))
population<-na.omit(population)
population<-select(population, 2:5)
population<-rename(population, Region=`TIME...2`) # Create maps for comparison rd vs students within years 2017, 2018, and 2019

all_data<- merge(all_data,population, by='Region')

all_data <- all_data%>%
  rename(Pop_2017=`2017`,
         Pop_2018=`2018`,
         Pop_2019=`2019`)
all_data<-all_data%>%  
  mutate(Student_Rate_2017=(Students_2017*1000/Pop_2017)*100,
         Student_Rate_2018=(Students_2018*1000/Pop_2017)*100,
         Student_Rate_2019=(Students_2019*1000/Pop_2019)*100)

```

## VIZUALIZATION OF SECTION 2

The main goal of this section is to analyze the relationship between
**GDP, R&D expenditures, and education** across Germany’s regions. The
map from the first section is enhanced by adding bubbles that represent
the number of students in higher education institutions. Six maps are
created to show these relationships over the course of three years.
First three maps show relationships between Regional GDP and Student
Rate, while the other three maps represent correlation between R&D
expenditure and Student Rate.

```{r,message=FALSE, include=TRUE}


# Create maps for comparison rd vs students within years 2017, 2018, and 2019


rd_stud_2017<-tm_shape(all_data)+
  tm_polygons(col='RD_2017', palette='YlOrRd', title='R&D expenditure(EUR mln)')+
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2017", col='#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha=0.7,
             title.size='Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1)+
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title ="R&D vs Students(2017)",
            bg.color = '#FFEFDB')

rd_stud_2018<-tm_shape(all_data)+
  tm_polygons(col='RD_2018', palette='YlOrRd', title='R&D expenditure(EUR mln)')+
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2018", col='#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha=0.7,
             title.size='Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1)+
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title ="R&D vs Students(2018)",
            bg.color = '#FFEFDB')

rd_stud_2019<-tm_shape(all_data)+
  tm_polygons(col='RD_2019', palette='YlOrRd', title='R&D expenditure(EUR mln)')+
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2019", col='#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha=0.7,
             title.size='Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1)+
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title ="R&D vs Students(2019)",
            bg.color = '#FFEFDB')

## Create maps for comparison gdp vs students within years 2017, 2018, and 2019

gdp_stud_2017<-tm_shape(all_data)+
  tm_polygons(col='GDP_2017', palette='YlGnBu', title='Regional GDP(EUR mln)')+
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2017", col='#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha=0.7,
             title.size='Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1)+
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title ="GDP vs Students(2017)",
            bg.color = '#E0FFFF')

gdp_stud_2018<-tm_shape(all_data)+
  tm_polygons(col='GDP_2018', palette='YlGnBu', title='Regional GDP(EUR mln)')+
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2018", col='#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha=0.7,
             title.size='Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1)+
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title ="GDP vs Students(2018)",
            bg.color = '#E0FFFF')


gdp_stud_2019 <- tm_shape(all_data) +
  tm_polygons(col = 'GDP_2019', palette = 'YlGnBu', title = 'Regional GDP(EUR mln)') +
  tm_bubbles(size = "Student_Rate_2019", col = '#d46ec9',
             scale = c(1.5,2,3,4),
             alpha = 0.7,
             title.size = 'Student rate per 10,000 people',
             border.col = "black", 
             border.lwd = 0.8,
             legend.size.show = TRUE,
             border.alpha = 1) +
  tm_layout(legend.title.size = 1.5,
            legend.text.size = 1,
            legend.outside = TRUE,
            main.title = "GDP vs Student(2019)",
            bg.color = '#E0FFFF')
```

## RESULTS OF VIZUALIZATION 2.1

```{r,message=FALSE, include=TRUE}
#arrange maps to show comparison

tmap_arrange(rd_stud_2017, gdp_stud_2017, ncol=2)
```

```{r,message=FALSE, include=TRUE}
tmap_arrange(rd_stud_2018, gdp_stud_2018, ncol=2)
```

```{r,message=FALSE, include=TRUE}
tmap_arrange(rd_stud_2019, gdp_stud_2019, ncol=2)
```

Interpreting regions like **Bavaria and Baden-Württemberg** in southern
Germany display higher GDP, highlighted with darker colors, while
eastern regions such as **Saxony and Brandenburg** show lighter shades,
indicating lower economic output, the distribution of R&D expenditures
is very similar to GDP patterns, with strong investments in southern
regions and lower values in the east. Also, student rate were
**unexpectedly small** in more developed regions with high investment in
innovation, such as North Rhine-Westphalia, Bavaria and
Baden-Württemberg it shows by smaller size of bubbles. The findings
suggest that student percentages and innovation are not necessarily
higher in wealthier regions. Developed regions may have lower
percentages of students due to large populations and diversified
economies. Less developed regions exhibit higher percentages as
education serves as a key lever for economic development. Faggian et al.
(2007) considered that students from economically weaker regions often
migrate to better-developed areas for higher education. The
socio-economic characteristics of regions affect the capacity to
transform R&D investments into human capital and innovation
(Bilbao‐Osorio, 2004). Less developed regions often emphasize the
expansion of higher education as a tool for economic growth because
other factors are limited. (Ozturk, 2008). Therefore, while education is
clearly an important factor in a country’s development, it will not
singlehandedly lead to significant economic growth (Chen, E., 2014)

## RESULTS OF VIZUALIZATION 2.2

```{r,message=FALSE, include=FALSE}
#save animation

tmap_animation(list(rd_stud_2017,rd_stud_2018,rd_stud_2019),filename = "Section_2_rd_education.gif",delay = 100,width = 800,height = 600)
tmap_animation(list(gdp_stud_2017,gdp_stud_2018,gdp_stud_2019),filename = "Section_2_gdp_education.gif",delay = 100,width = 800,height = 600)

```

![](Section_2_rd_education.gif) ![](Section_2_gdp_education.gif)

## CONCLUSION OF SECTION 2

Our hypothesis that regions with higher GDP and innovation would have
more students was **not fully supported by the results.** While southern
regions with higher economic output and innovation investments do
exhibit larger economies, they also have a lower percentage of students.
This suggests that other factors, such as population size and economic
structure, may influence these patterns. The spatial distribution of
GDP, R&D and education shows regional inequalities in Germany. Also we
find out that southern regions consistently outperform others, while the
eastern regions lag behind.

**References:**

[1] Ozturk, I. (2008). The role of education in economic development: a
theoretical perspective. Available at SSRN 1137541.
<https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=1137541>

[2] Grant, C. (2017). The contribution of education to economic growth.
K4D Helpdesk Report. Brighton, UK: Institute of Development Studies, 25.
<https://www.academia.edu/90176425/The_contribution_of_education_to_economic_growth>

[3] Bilbao‐Osorio, B., & Rodríguez‐Pose, A. (2004). From R&D to
innovation and economic growth in the EU. Growth and Change, 35(4),
434-455.https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1468-2257.2004.00256.x?casa_token=a5YRTSDXFGEAAAAA:7t_dn0IzqY83zdpKyr16jzdAmwNgbhRqrIKTLATCHZlMBPFLQX_AqzGM5ENQ8FPAU7RflLktG4SbE7nu2A

[4] Chen, E. (2014). The Impact of Education on Economic Growth
(Doctoral dissertation, Stern School of Business New
York).https://www.stern.nyu.edu/sites/default/files/assets/documents/Emil%20Chen_Thesis_Honors%202014.pdf

[5] Oketch, M., McCowan, T., & Schendel, R. (2014). The impact of
tertiary education on development: A rigorous literature
review.https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/10068500/

[6] Faggian, A., McCann, P., & Sheppard, S. (2007). Human capital,
higher education and graduate migration: an analysis of Scottish and
Welsh students. Urban Studies, 44(13), 2511-2528.
<https://www.jstor.org/stable/pdf/43197599.pdf>

## SECTION 3

The third section is measuring how distances in our map_data dataset
impact a particular variable. In our case, we have two variables,
Research and Development (monetary investment in research and
development across regions) and Education (the number of educated
individuals (in thousands) across regions) in Germany. First of all, we
made a data preparation as in section 1 and 2. Thus, the final cleaned
version is **map_data**, which we will use. It has sorted necessary
variables that needed as: **gdpmln:** GDP in millions for each region
**rdmln:** R&D expenditure in millions for each region **eduthous:**
Education levels in thousands **geometry:** Spatial geometry of regions
**Ii_gdp and Ii_rdmln:** Local Moran’s I values for GDP and R&D

Additionally, there is a step that is the most significant one for
conducting the analysis - downloading the libraries for each of the
required steps.

## DATA PREPARATION

```{r,warning=FALSE}

education<-read_excel('Population (aged 15 and over).xlsx',sheet='Used')
RD<-read_excel('R&D expenditure and staff.xlsx')
gdp<-read_excel('gdp.xlsx',sheet='Sheet 1',skip=7)

```

## PRE-PROCESS GDP DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}

######################################################### GDP ACROSS REGIONS


gdp_clean<-select(gdp,TIME,starts_with('20'))
gdp_clean<-rename(gdp_clean,Region=TIME)
gdp_clean<-na.omit(gdp_clean)

gdp_clean_period<-select(gdp_clean,'Region','2017','2018','2019')
gdp_test<-gdp_clean_period

region_to_state <- list(
  "Baden-Württemberg_" = c("Stuttgart", "Karlsruhe", "Freiburg", "Tübingen"),
  "Bayern_" = c("Oberbayern", "Niederbayern", "Oberpfalz", "Oberfranken", "Mittelfranken", "Unterfranken", "Schwaben"),
  "Berlin_" = "Berlin",
  "Brandenburg_" = "Brandenburg",
  "Bremen_" = "Bremen",
  "Hamburg_" = "Hamburg",
  "Hessen_" = c("Darmstadt", "Gießen", "Kassel"),
  "Mecklenburg-Vorpommern_"="Mecklenburg-Vorpommern",
  "Niedersachsen_" = c("Braunschweig", "Hannover", "Lüneburg", "Weser-Ems"),
  "Nordrhein-Westfalen_" = c("Köln", "Münster", "Detmold", "Arnsberg"),
  "Rheinland-Pfalz_" = c("Rheinhessen-Pfalz","Koblenz","Trier"),
  "Saarland_" = "Saarland",
  "Sachsen_" = c("Dresden", "Chemnitz", "Leipzig"),
  "Sachsen-Anhalt_" = "Sachsen-Anhalt",
  "Schleswig-Holstein_" = "Schleswig-Holstein",
  "Thüringen_" = "Thüringen")


region_to_state_vec <- unlist(region_to_state)

gdp_test$FederalState <- names(region_to_state_vec)[match(gdp_test$Region, region_to_state_vec)]

gdp_test<-gdp_test%>%
  separate(FederalState, into = c("FederalState", "number"), sep = "_")
gdp_test$number=NULL

gdp_test_long<-pivot_longer(gdp_test,cols=starts_with('20'),
                                      values_to='Value',
                                      names_to='Year')
gdp_test_long$Region<-NULL                            

gdp_test_sum<-gdp_test_long%>%
  group_by(FederalState,Year)%>%
  summarize(TotalGDP=sum(Value,na.rm=TRUE))

gdp_clean_full<-pivot_wider(gdp_test_sum,names_from='Year',
                            values_from='TotalGDP')
gdp_clean_full<-rename(gdp_clean_full,Region=FederalState)


```

## PRE-PROCESS EDUCATION DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}
####################################################### EDUCATION ACROSS REGIONS


education_filtered<-filter(education,Sex=='Total')
education_filtered$Sex<-NULL
education_filtered$Total<-NULL
education_filtered$`Specialised and engineering school certific. (GDR)`<-as.numeric(education_filtered$`Specialised and engineering school certific. (GDR)`)
education_filtered$Year<-as.numeric(education_filtered$Year)
education_filtered<-rename(education_filtered,'Trade_and_technical_school_certificate'='Trade and technical school certificate')
education_filtered<-rename(education_filtered,'Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR'='Specialised and engineering school certific. (GDR)')
education_filtered<-rename(education_filtered,"Doctor"="Doctor's degree")

library(VIM)
aggr(education_filtered)

library(mice)
library(foreign)
imp<-mice(education_filtered,method='pmm',maxit=10,m=1)
?complete()
education_filtered<-complete(imp,action='long',include=FALSE)

education_filtered$.imp<-NULL
education_filtered$.id<-NULL

education_filtered<-education_filtered%>%
  mutate(Total=Trade_and_technical_school_certificate+Specialised_and_engineering_school_certificate_GDR+Bachelor+Master+Diploma+Doctor)
education_filtered<-education_filtered%>%
  select(Year,Region,Total)

education_clean_full<-education_filtered%>%
  pivot_wider(names_from='Year',
              values_from='Total')

```

## PRE-PROCESS R&D DATA

```{r,warning=FALSE}

######################################################### RESEARCH AND DEVELOPMENT ACROSS REGIONS



RD_filtered<-na.omit(RD)
RD_filtered<-filter(RD_filtered,Unit=='EUR mn')
RD_filtered$Unit<-NULL
RD_filtered$Type<-NULL

RD_filtered<-pivot_longer(RD_filtered, cols=('Baden-Württemberg':'Thüringen'),
                          values_to='Value',
                          names_to='Region')
RD_filtered<-pivot_wider(RD_filtered,
                         names_from='Sector',
                         values_from='Value')

RD_filtered$Gov<-RD_filtered$`Government, private non-profit institutions`
RD_filtered$`Government, private non-profit institutions`<-NULL
RD_total<-select(RD_filtered,Year,Region,Total)

RD_clean<-pivot_wider(RD_total,
                         names_from='Year',
                         values_from='Total')


```

## MERGING EVERYTHING

```{r,warning=FALSE}
############################################################# MERGING AND PRE-PROCESS FULL DATA

full_data<-merge(gdp_clean_full,RD_clean,by='Region')
full_data<-merge(full_data,education_clean_full,by='Region')

full_data<-full_data%>%
  rename('2017_gdpmln'='2017.x')%>%
  rename('2018_gdpmln'='2018.x')%>%
  rename('2019_gdpmln'='2019.x')%>%
  rename('2017_rdmln'='2017.y')%>%
  rename('2018_rdmln'='2018.y')%>%
  rename('2019_rdmln'='2019.y')%>%
  rename('2017_eduthous'='2017')%>%
  rename('2018_eduthous'='2018')%>%
  rename('2019_eduthous'='2019')

full_data<-pivot_longer(full_data,cols=starts_with('20'),
                        names_to='Year',
                        values_to='Value')
full_data<-separate(full_data,col='Year',into=c('Year','Type'),sep='_')
full_data<-pivot_wider(full_data,
                       names_from='Type',
                       values_from='Value')

############################################################# UPLOADING MAP AND MERGING WITH FULL DATA

library(sf)

germany<-st_read('gadm41_DEU_2.shp')
germany<-germany<-rename(germany,Region=NAME_1)

map_data<-merge(germany,full_data,by='Region')
map_data<-select(map_data,Region,Year,gdpmln,rdmln,eduthous,geometry)

map_data<-st_as_sf(map_data)
```

Secondly, for better understanding the data, we will visualize the plot
distribution. The first plot will be regarding the variable Education,
while the second one is R&D respectively. By illustrating choropleth we
see the distribution of education levels (eduthous) among regions in
Germany. Their darker shades show regions with higher education such as
urban areas, whereas lighter ones represent lower education levels.
Furthermore, **quantile classification** ensures that regions are
divided into equal-sized groups for a clearer comparison.

```{r,warning=FALSE}
colnames(map_data)
#plot the distribution of variables (Education)
tm_shape(map_data) + 
  tm_fill("eduthous", palette = "Reds",   
          style = "quantile", title = "Education (thousands)") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.4) +
  tm_compass(position = c("left", "top")) + 
  tm_layout(
    title = "Education Distribution by Region", 
    legend.text.size = 0.5,
    legend.title.size = 0.7,
    legend.position = c("right", "bottom"), 
    legend.width = 1.5, 
    frame = FALSE
  )
```

The second bubble plot illustrates the R&D expenditure, with larger and
darker blue bubbles **higher R&D investments**, while the opposite for
the lower investments as regions with lack of infrastructure and
funding..

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#plot the distribution for another variable (R&D)
tm_shape(map_data) + 
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.4) +  
  tm_bubbles(size = "rdmln", col = "rdmln", 
             palette = "Blues", style = "quantile", 
             legend.size.show = FALSE, title.col = "R&D Expenditure (Million)") +
  tm_layout(
    legend.text.size = 1.1,
    legend.title.size = 1.4,
    frame = FALSE
  )
```

Third plot is **2 in 1**, which represents two variables in one plot,
where we can conclude that regions with higher education levels often
align with regions that invest more in R&D. This suggests that a more
educated population contributes to innovation and research activities
and educational infrastructure supports industries that prioritize R&D
investments.

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#2 in 1
tm_shape(map_data) + 
  tm_fill("eduthous", palette = "Reds",  
          style = "quantile", title = "Education Levels (Thousand)") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.4) +  
  tm_shape(map_data) + 
  tm_bubbles(size = "rdmln", col = "rdmln",  
             palette = "Blues", style = "quantile", 
             legend.size.show = FALSE, title.col = "R&D Expenditure", 
             border.col = "black", border.lwd = 0.1, border.alpha = 0.1) +
  tm_layout(
    legend.text.size = 0.8,
    legend.title.size = 1.1,
    frame = FALSE
  )
```

## AUTO-SPATIAL ANALYSIS: NEIGHBOURS

The analysis explores whether regions with similar education levels, or
R&D expenditures are geographically clustered. To achieve this, spatial
relationships between regions are defined using two criteria: the
"queen" criterion, where regions sharing any boundary point are
neighbors, and the "rook" criterion, where only regions sharing an edge
qualify as neighbors. The “poly2nb” function is used to establish these
neighbor relationships, while the “nb2listw” function converts them into
a spatial weight matrix. This matrix quantifies the influence of
neighboring regions on one another, forming the foundation for
statistical tests like Moran’s I, which measures global spatial
autocorrelation.

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#let's find neighbours

library(tmap)
library(sf)
library(spdep)
neighbours <- poly2nb(map_data) 
neighbours

```

**Output:**

Number of regions: 1209 #number of direct spatial connections exist
between regions

Number of nonzero links: 21354 #proportion of the total possible spatial
connections

Percentage nonzero weights: 1.460921 

Average number of links: 17.66253  #each region has around 17.6
neighbors

Weights style: W (the weights matrix is row-standardized. Each row sums
to 1, ensuring that the influence of neighbors is proportionally
distributed)

Weights constants summary:

     n      nn   S0       S1       S2

W 1209 1461681 1209 157.2312 5004.012\
Row-standardized weights show that the spatial relationships are
comparable across regions regardless of the number of neighbors. The
summary statistics (S0, S1, S2) reflect the structure and scaling of the
spatial relationships.

## AUTO-SPATIAL ANALYSIS: SPATIAL AUTOCORRELATION

This section applies Moran's I statistics to examine spatial
autocorrelation in R&D expenditures across regions. Global Moran’s I,
computed using **moran.test**, quantifies whether similar values cluster
spatially. A significant result suggests that high or low R&D values are
not randomly distributed but exhibit clustering patterns.

The Moran plot visualizes this relationship by comparing each region’s
R&D values against the spatially lagged values of its neighbors,
offering insights into positive or negative spatial autocorrelation.

Local Moran’s I, calculated with **localmoran**, identifies specific
regions contributing to the observed global pattern. Regions with
significant local Moran statistics are potential hotspots (clusters of
high values) or cold spots (clusters of low values).

This analysis helps pinpoint spatial patterns, aiding in understanding
regional disparities and informing policy decisions for equitable
resource distribution and development strategies.

```{r,warning=FALSE}
plot(map_data, border = 'pink')
```

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#spatial autocorrelation

neighbours2 <- poly2nb(map_data, queen = FALSE) 
neighbours2

listw <- nb2listw(neighbours2) 
listw
```

**Moran I test under randomisation**

data:  map_data\$rdmln  

weights: listw    

Moran I statistic standard deviate = 87.891, p-value \< 2.2e-16

alternative hypothesis: greater

**Moran I statistic       Expectation          Variance**

**0.9049065164     -0.0008278146      0.0001061966**

A value of 0.9 indicates a very high positive spatial autocorrelation.
Regions with similar R&D expenditure levels tend to cluster
geographically.

**Standard deviate:** 87.891 is the z-score for Moran's I. A very high
z-score indicates strong deviation from randomness, further confirming
significant spatial clustering

#### The expected Moran's I value under the null hypothesis (random distribution). This is close to zero, which aligns with no spatial autocorrelation.

#### The variance of Moran's I under the null hypothesis, used to compute the test statistic.

**Null Hypothesis:** There is no spatial autocorrelation (R&D values are
randomly distributed).

**Alternative Hypothesis:** Spatial autocorrelation exists (R&D values
are spatially clustered). **p-value \< 2.2e-16:** reject the null
hypothesis.

**Conclusion: There is significant positive spatial autocorrelation in
R&D expenditure.**

## AUTO-SPATIAL ANALYSIS: MORAN TEST

This section integrates the calculated local Moran's I statistics with
the polygon shapefile of regional boundaries to visualize spatial
autocorrelation patterns geographically. The results from localmoran are
merged into the map_dataobject using cbind, ensuring that each region's
polygon is enriched with its respective local Moran statistic.

The visualization, created using the tm_shape and tm_fill functions,
displays regions colored based on their local Moran's I values. A
quantile classification method ensures an even distribution of values
across categories, making patterns easy to interpret. Regions with
higher or lower values are highlighted, reflecting clusters of similar
R&D expenditure levels.

The map provides an intuitive spatial representation of local
clustering, with the borders overlaid for clarity. The visualization
facilitates identifying specific regions contributing to global spatial
patterns, aiding in spatial decision-making and policy interventions.

```{r}
# global spatial autocorrelation

moran.test(map_data$rdmln, listw)
```

## AUTO-SPATIAL ANALYSIS: MORAN PLOT

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#moran plot

moran <- moran.plot(map_data$rdmln, listw = nb2listw(neighbours2, style = "W"))
```

## AUTO-SPATIAL ANALYSIS: LOCAL MORAN OUTPUT & MORAN MAP

```{r,warning=FALSE}
#local moran output

local <- localmoran(x = map_data$rdmln, listw = nb2listw(neighbours2, style = "W"))

# binds results to our polygon shapefile
moran.map <- cbind(map_data, local)
# maps the results
tm_shape(moran.map) + tm_fill(col = "Ii", style = "quantile", title = "local moran statistic") +
  tm_borders(alpha = 0.4) +
  tm_layout(legend.outside = TRUE)

```

**References:**

[1] Kiese, M. (2019). Regional cluster policies in Germany: challenges,
impacts and evaluation practices. The Journal of Technology Transfer,
44(6), 1698-1719.

[2] Haak, R., Gautam, V., Walsh, S. T., & White, C. G. (2014). The role
of economic cluster perspectives in regional economic development. World
Technopolis Review, 3(1), 17-29.

[3] Szendi, D. CONVERGENCE AND SPATIAL AUTOCORRELATION IN
GERMANY–SPECIFIC GDP INEQUALITIES 30 YEARS AFTER THE FALL OF THE BERLIN
WALL.

[4] Zierahn, U. (2012). The importance of spatial autocorrelation for
regional employment growth in Germany. Jahrbuch für
Regionalwissenschaft, 32, 19-43.

## DATASETS:

All datasets and information were taken from the official Germany
statistics website:
<https://www-genesis.destatis.de/datenbank/online/statistics> And at
official website of the European Union(Eurostat):
<https://ec.europa.eu/eurostat>
